speaker: interviewee
text: Might listen or watch this is that no human being should ever have to go through what I went through. It's-- no matter how much I-- my experiences, no matter how much I tell, you still can't tell it what it was. Because it was much more horrible, much more worse, and everything like that. And everything I said is the truth, and much more. And if people have to go through-- should only go through by watching this, and not physically, nothing, and things like that. And I hope something like this will never happen. And I don't think it will ever happen. I have-- I feel confidence it will never happen again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That would be my message.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you, you mentioned that, just going back a little bit, you mentioned that you  were in Tennessee. Am I correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And then when I asked about your wife, you met her in California. Tell me how you got from Tennessee to California.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, well, when I was-- I came to Tennessee in 1949. And in 1950, I was drafted in the United States Army. I spent two years in the army. And then in between 1949 and 1952, when I was in the army, my sister-- her name is Sabina-- she came to the United States in, I think, 1950. She was sponsored by her husband's cousin or relative. And they were in South Bend, Indiana. And then they moved to California.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I got out of the army, I came to visit my sister. They lived here in Culver City. So when I came to visit her, I called the old place where I worked. He wanted me to come back. And I even wound up-- I was working back in Ohio for two years for my old boss, when I worked for-- when I first started in Tennessee, the same man. But then I didn't like it, and I came out to California, too, at my sister's. And I got a job here in California.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how I remained here in Culver City. And I met my wife, on a-- well, it was just kind of like a friend of mine that worked with me, his brother had a little fur store, factory. And my wife went to buy a collar for a sweater, a fur collar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this guy says, are you single? She said, yes. Do you have a boyfriend? She says, no. She said, I have just the guy for you. Because we used to play cards together, something. So he called me up, and he says, I have a girlfriend for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I want you to talk to her. So I talked to her. And we made a date, and the rest is history.

speaker: interviewer
text: And anybody else from the family? You mentioned your sister. Who else was able to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, yeah, and it's a funny thing, you know. Going back there-- when I was in the army, OK, my sister, she came over right away, you know. But when I was in-- when I was left in the United States Army, when I was in the army, my other sister came over.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Helen. And they came to the United States, to the-- to New York. And at that time, I was in the army, but I was able to meet them at the boat as a GI. [LAUGHS] And when I met them at the boat as a GI, you know, when they went through the customs, you know-- and I was able to go to the-- to go to customs, I was here to meet them, and tell them. So when they got to the customs, they just pushed us right through. And I was able to help them-- I was able to-- they found a place in New York. And it all went so fast. And then I went back to the camp-- I mean, I was in the army. And my brother came over six months later. And I was able to go back to New York and meet them too. And it was quite a nice reunion, even though that-- I was already-- And then after spending two years in the army, going back, I-- after two years, I was discharged. Eisenhower was president at that time. And he said, anybody that served in the United States army for a period of six months or longer, and was honorable discharge, was entitled to apply for citizenship papers. And I applied, and it was in the small town of Portman, Ohio. And I got my citizenship paper right away because I was already a war veteran. And that's-- yeah, and then afterwards, when I came to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the date you became an American  citizen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's odd.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] But I remember it was a small town, you know. And when I went to the office to apply for citizen, in that small town I met, one-- it was a Jewish man, he was a city councilman. And I was managing a jewelry store-- in a small town. So you met quite a few people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told him about it that I'm going to become a citizen, and I need a witness. And I knew him for a little while. He said, sure. I'll be your witness. And then I also met a sergeant, in the police department, a sergeant. He used come in. I used to sell him something. We became good friends. So I says, I need two witnesses. I need a witness. He said, I'll be your witness. So when we get in the office, and I-- and they say, I have to pay $10 for the papers. He said no, he doesn't have to pay because he's a veteran, you know. So he said everything is fine. You passed everything. But you don't have any witnesses. Don't you know, you should have two witnesses? I says, well I have two witnesses, because they knew the people over there. So they start standing with me as a witness. They talked to the other people. So they say, who's your witnesses? I says, well, Sergeant so and so, and this councilman. Those are your witnesses? I said, yes. OK. [LAUGHS] I had good witnesses. And your occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I-- when I first came over here, I wanted to-- my brother-in-law that came over-- which is my sister-- I mean Opperman. He was a watchmaker in the old country. And he was teaching me how to be a watchmaker. He says, because when you get to the United States, you got to have a trade. What are you going to do if you don't have a trade?

speaker: interviewer
text: So he taught me watch repair. And when I first came to Tennessee, I was working as watch repair for about, oh, three months or so. I never liked it. But it got me a job. And then I didn't work because I managed a store and everything, jewelry department. And what's your occupation now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a-- my occupation now, I have a jewelry store. I worked for somebody for 26 years. And 14 years ago, I opened my own store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that we didn't talk about that you would like to mention now, before we introduce your lovely family and pictures of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm a happy man. I-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I'm just a happy-- I have a lovely wife. I have lovely three kids. I have-- I'm a fortunate man. I-- I-- I found two sisters and a brother. A lot of people didn't find anybody. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we're very close. And I'm just-- In-- in-- in my experiences and in all these things, sometimes I know I went through them. I never forget them, but sometimes they seem like a dream. It doesn't seem like it's real. Because if you thought-- if you thought it was a real, if you think of the things that you would-- that you went through, you don't think you could-- you could-- you could manage. You don't think you could survive. That's why I always say, there is no limit-- oh, one more thing I want to say. I want to say the human body is stronger than anything in the world. It's stronger than iron. It's stronger than steel. It can survive more than anything in the world. And you don't think you can survive, but you can. That's why I always say, there's no limit to the good. And there's no limit to the bad. If somebody say, well, business is quiet. I say, it could be worse. If it's good, it could be better, you know. And I'm-- I-- I just feel great. I'm healthy. And-- and I have wonderful kids. I have-- my youngest one, unfortunately, has a disability. He's in a wheelchair. He wasn't born in a wheelchair, but he is. And it's difficult for us. And whenever I think it's difficult for me, I know it's twice as hard for him. And I try to make the best out of it, and that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: One quick question I didn't ask you, were you bar mitzvahed? I meant to ask you that.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- I wasn't. I mean, I remember I laid [NON-ENGLISH]-- I laid [NON-ENGLISH]. But I don't remember being bar mitzvahed like they have-- like my kids had their bar mitzvahs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well it was during the war, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah-- I remember they were teaching me how to lay [NON-ENGLISH]. And I remember that-- I laid [NON-ENGLISH], But that's about all I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much, Mr. Price.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's Maurie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maurie, thank you Maurie. We'll now see your lovely family and pictures. Thank you so much. Sure, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you introduce your lovely family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, to my left here, it's David. To my right, it's my wife, Shirley.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shirley Price. Her maiden name was Levine. My daughter, Debra, Debra Beth, Price also. She's still single. My son, my oldest one, Michael, Michael Alan. Also Price, and he's still single, too. And that's about it, and my family [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, is there anything you'd like to say about your father, how you feel about all the experiences he's gone through, or about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my dad is an amazing human being to have lived through and endured what he experienced. That's something I can't even imagine. I hope I'll never imagine it. I-- I think he's incredible, and I'm glad you're doing this project. I hope by continuing to talk to people who survived the Holocaust, we can teach other people and prevent anything like this from occurring again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Thank you. Now I think we'll show some lovely pictures of the whole family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel, like my son, that my husband is an amazing person. And I'm glad he survived. Because this way, I got to marry a wonderful man and have three beautiful children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody else, Debra, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as my mom and brother just said, I mean, my father's been wonderful. And he's-- he's just wonderful to know. And I'm just so grateful that he's always been here to be my-- my father. And I don't think I can say much more. I'm probably going to start crying. So I'm going to stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else, anybody else would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just read an article about my dad about how the world to survive, that they're made to survive. It's amaze-- there was-- he survived like a warrior spirit. And my dad's a warrior. And then, I guess that's how I-- one of the ways I battle lots of life, since I'm in a wheelchair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else anybody would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm not going to say anymore. I said enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said enough. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not going to say anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. I'll go on and show some pictures. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother. This is a passport picture. It was taken during the war. I don't know exactly when-- whether it was 1942. I don't remember exactly for what reason we wanted to get some passport pictures, maybe try to get out of the country or something. But that's what this picture is.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Ida.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kool. It's Price. But her maiden name is Kool. That's my father. And again, this picture was taken for a passport. It must have been taken the same time, about 1942. For what reason or whatever it is, didn't make it. But that's-- that's my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Manella, Manella Price.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how you got these two pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: No I don't. I checked with my sister, and she says that I was supposed to have gotten it. The only thing that I can remember that I might have gotten it if some relative, not for myself, some relative that might have had it during the war. And I think I remember vaguely, but I don't remember exactly, some cousins or something like that. That's my sister. She is the oldest sister. Her name was Brandl-- also Price. She was married a short while during the war. Again, this picture was taken for a passport. I don't even remember her married name. And she didn't survive the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were these pictures taken, what town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wolbrom.

speaker: interviewer
text: All three?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And that's me in person, starting off with Moishe, Moniak, Maurie and Morris Price.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was also taken during the war. I think it was 1942, also for a passport. I guess we were hoping to get out or something. But that's about all I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wolbrom, in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: 14-- 14, 13, or 14. And that is my older brother, David, David Price. This picture was also taken in Wolbrom, Poland, and I think about the same time. Because I guess we all took pictures for passport pictures. And he didn't survive. And that's my youngest son, David, named after him. And that's me again. It was taken in Germany, in Munich in 1946. At that time, at one time I was called Max, because in Germany-- so it's still Moishe, Maurie, or Moniak.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Munich at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Munich from 1945 to 1949, four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Munich, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maurie.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, fine, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, and this is Chayim. He's one of my friends that we survived Auschwitz, all the camps together. He lives in Israel right now. This picture was taken after the war in Israel. And he had sent it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember approximately when?

speaker: interviewee
text: '46, or something like that-- '47. OK, this is my sister Helen's wedding. This was taken in December, 1946. My sister and her husband, Liam. To the left of her, it's myself, Maurie, and to the left of me is my sister Sabina.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Rubin, Liam Rubin, my sister Helen Rubin, Moishe Price, Moniak, and my sister Sabina Opperman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1946, December 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munich, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is me again when I was in the United States Army. This was taken in 1951 in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was stationed in Mannheim, near Heidelberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And please-- your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maurie Price. OK, and this is my wife, radiant as always, and myself. And we were going-- we had a black tie affair. I don't know exactly where we were going, but it was like in 1984 or '85 here in Culver City.

speaker: interviewer
text: Names, did you mention?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maurie and Shirley Price. And these are my kids. The one in the middle is David. And it was his birthday party, which was December '94. And to his right is Michael, his oldest brother, and to his left is Debra, his older sister. And Michael Alan Price, David Jay Price, and Debra Beth Price. OK, and this is a picture of my family. It's David's bar mitzvah. He was 13. He is in the center. I guess you can tell-- he's the star. OK, right in the back of him is his older brother Michael. To his right is his mom, Shirley Price. To her right is Debra Price. And to her right is a nephew, Irv Rubin. Next is my sister, Sabina Opperman, and her husband Liam Opperman. They're both survivors, camp survivors. And going back to the other side, there's myself, Morris. Next is my sister, Helen, her husband Liam, my brother Michael. Liam and Michael and my sister, they're all Holocaust survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about their last names?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, Michael is Price. Liam is Rubin. And my sister, her married name is Helen Rubin. And in the back, to the right, in the back of my daughter, is Irv Rubin. He is the son of my sister, Helen, and Liam Rubin. And the rest of them-- is my son Michael, and my daughter and my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their last names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last name, my wife is Shirley Price. My daughter is Debra Price. My son is Michael Price, and my sister to the right of my daughter is Sabina Opperman and Liam Opperman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the bar mitzvah boy is wearing a dark blue suit--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the bar mitzvah boy is David Jay Price, the bar mitzvah boy.
